context.description: German nonfiction prose argues about pattern recognition while driving on the autobahn, especially noticing number plates that resemble 666. It weighs an alternative explanation based on automatic eye movements and rejects it as hard to verify without a tracker. The passage concludes by using deduction and risk-based observation of traffic behavior as evidence.
normalization_notes: Title inferred from source file name.
Source text is German; normalized summary translated into English.

rows.paragraph_text: Only about 5% were visually similar patterns such as 999, 669, or other configurations close to 666.

An alternative explanation is considered: continuously scanning the visual field for familiar patterns and immediately looking at license plates when they enter view. This would also require constant unconscious eye movement and selective forgetting, which the author treats as possible but difficult to prove.

The author says the only reliable check would be a tracker recording eye movements and traffic focus, but time and money prevent building such a test setup. Deduction is therefore used, with highway traffic situations serving as observable conditions for judging attention and perception.

content.free_text: book_page book_body_content chapter_section German nonfiction prose about pattern recognition on the autobahn, alternative explanations involving unconscious eye movements, and deductive observation of attention.
